name: algorithms-to-live-by
description: Brian Christian and Tom Griffiths' guide to using computer science algorithms for everyday decisions. Covers: stopping when to commit, exploring vs. exploiting, organizing space, managing time, handling uncertainty, and strategic thinking.
useCase: Apply computer science solutions to everyday human problems — from finding a partner to organizing your closet to making better decisions under uncertainty.
knownRisks: Algorithms are approximations of reality, not prescriptions. Real human decisions involve emotions and values that no algorithm captures.
Some algorithms require precise inputs (time horizon, discount rate) that users may not know. Use estimates, not guesses.
The optimal stopping rule (37%) assumes random ordering. Real life has patterns — adjust accordingly.
